role_description: Junior Instrument Technician assisting senior techs and techs on industrial construction projects. Carries materials, pulls instrument cable, runs tubing, supports installations, maintains housekeeping, and learns the instrumentation trade under supervision.
report_focus: When structuring this person's reports, look for: tasks assisted with today, materials moved or staged, safety observations, work area conditions, what they learned, and any issues to report.

vocabulary.description: Basic instrumentation terms a junior tech encounters while assisting senior techs.

safety_rules: All personnel must wear required PPE at all times: hard hat, safety glasses, steel-toe boots, FR clothing
Report all incidents, near-misses, and unsafe conditions immediately to your supervisor
Never work on any equipment without direct supervision from a qualified technician
Fall protection is required at heights of 6 feet or more — 100% tie-off at all times
Maintain 3 points of contact when climbing ladders
Keep work areas clean and organized — good housekeeping prevents injuries
Know the location of the nearest fire extinguisher, eye wash station, and first aid kit
Never bypass or remove safety guards, barriers, or warning signs
All scaffolds must be inspected and tagged before use — never use a red-tagged scaffold
Attend daily toolbox talks and JSA reviews before starting work
Report any damaged tools or equipment immediately — do not use defective equipment
Be aware of overhead hazards and never stand under suspended loads
Never operate equipment you are not trained and authorized to use
Wear cut-resistant gloves when handling tubing, cable, or sharp materials
Use proper lifting techniques — get help for loads over 50 lbs
Stay clear of energized equipment and pressurized lines at all times
Always follow the buddy system — never work alone in remote areas
Know your emergency evacuation routes and muster point
Drug and alcohol policies are strictly enforced — fit for duty at all times
All workers must complete site orientation before starting work
tools_and_equipment: Tube cutter — inspect blade condition before each use
Tube bender — use correct die size for tubing OD, never use pliers
Deburring tool — deburr all tubing cuts before installing fittings
Swaging tool — for prepping tube ends per fitting requirements
Wire strippers — use correct gauge setting to avoid nicking conductors
Cable cutters — use proper rated cutters for cable size
Crimping tool — verify crimp die matches terminal size
Screwdrivers — flathead and Phillips, various sizes
Allen wrenches — metric and standard sets
Adjustable wrench — for general fitting work, not on compression fittings
Open-end wrenches — various sizes for tubing and fittings
Tape measure — 25-foot minimum
Level — torpedo level for instrument mounting
Multimeter — basic voltage and continuity checks under supervision
Fish tape — for pulling cable through conduit
Pull string — for cable routing in tray and conduit
Cable ties and markers — for proper cable management and labeling
Hand truck and dolly — for moving heavy instruments and materials
Step ladder and extension ladder — inspect before each use, proper setup angles
Gang box — keep tools organized and secured
language_notes: Workers may mix English and Spanish. Technical terms are usually said in English. Common Spanish field terms: 'tubo' (tubing), 'cable', 'caja' (box/junction box), 'herramienta' (tool), 'material', 'escalera' (ladder), 'casco' (hard hat), 'lentes' (safety glasses).
